questions.question: What latency makes a voice assistant feel alive vs broken?
options: Anything under 5 seconds is fine
Human turn-taking is ~230ms; above 500ms feels robotic, above 1500ms feels broken — the 2026 hear→understand→respond→speak budget totals ~400ms with streaming ASR at 150ms as the biggest line item
Latency is irrelevant if quality is high
Exactly 1000ms is the sweet spot
questions.explanation: The 10x improvement from 2022's 2500ms cascades came from three techniques: streaming everywhere, asynchronous pipelining with partial results, and interruptible generation.

questions.question: What is a ring buffer and why does real-time audio need one?
options: A circular microphone array

A fixed-size circular buffer where the producer thread writes frames and the consumer reads — preventing allocations in the hot path; size ≈ max-latency × sample-rate (a 2s ring at 16 kHz = 32,000 samples)
A buffer shaped like the Opus logo
An unbounded queue with backpressure
questions.explanation: Real-time audio flows as fixed 20ms blocks (320 samples at 16 kHz), and everything downstream must keep that cadence — allocation pauses or GC in the hot path become audible glitches.

questions.question: What does VAD contribute to the pipeline, and what's the 2026 default?
options: It amplifies quiet speakers
It gates downstream work when nobody is speaking — Silero VAD 4.0 runs under 1ms per 30ms frame on CPU, so the expensive ASR/LLM stages only fire on actual speech
It translates voice to commands directly
It removes the need for a microphone
questions.explanation: VAD is also the interruption trigger: barge-in detection, TTS stop, and LLM-output discard must all complete within ~100ms or the user perceives a deaf assistant.

questions.question: What is the jitter buffer's trade-off in networked voice?
options: Bigger is always better
Packets arrive out of order or late; the jitter buffer reorders and smooths — too small causes audible gaps, too large adds latency; 60-80ms is typical over WebRTC Opus transport
It compresses audio for storage
It only matters for video calls
questions.explanation: WebRTC Opus (20ms frames, 48 kHz, adaptive 8-128 kbps) is the browser/mobile standard, with LiveKit, Daily.co, and Pion as the 2026 voice-app stacks.

questions.question: Which gotchas bite Python real-time audio pipelines?
options: Python cannot play audio at all

GIL thread contention starving the audio thread (use C-callback libraries, keep Python off the hot path), resampling latency mid-pipeline, TTS cold-start (warm Kokoro with a dummy run), and missing echo cancellation letting TTS re-trigger ASR on the bot's own voice
Only file-format mismatches
Excessive RAM usage from numpy
questions.explanation: The echo problem is the sneakiest: without AEC (WebRTC AEC3 is the open default), the assistant hears itself talk and transcribes it as user input.
